data.title: Gold Has A New Buyer, A Different Kind Of Central Bank
data.description:
In October, I wrote about the structural changes in the buyers of gold, and attributed much of the rise in the price of gold to ex-U.S. forces, namely central banks and the speculation of de-dollarizing economies (which is likely folly, but I don't dictate what other countries do).

Recent data has come out that changes my perspective on where the gold demand is coming from (it's still not the United States!), and it has made me more bullish than I was before. The structural changes are still underway.

Foreign Central Bank Demand Is Still Strong

Don't get me wrong here; I still believe that the major drivers of long-term gold demand are coming from outside the United States.

We know that it's not U.S. institutions (well, they are speculating, but not really allocating). We can see that in conservative institutions like family offices, allocation to gold is abysmally low.

I covered this in "Smart Money Is Reallocating." Here's my favorite chart from that article. It shows family office average allocation changes from the last two years.

Even though alternatives are on the rise, this hasn't included commodities. U.S. institutions are still eschewing direct gold exposure, despite its doubling in value over that time.

But foreign central banks, notably the People's Bank of China ("PBOC"), have been importing gold like nobody's business. PBOC's share of gold in its reserves is now the highest in modern history.

I detailed these purchases in October, but it's notable that since then, we've only seen further increases in purchases. Of course, this data is a little noisy, because it's based on IMF reporting, and some countries tend not to report fully (looking at you, PBOC). However, the trend remains intact for the year, and sales of gold have dropped off despite the paper profits many of these central banks are sitting on.

Following September, October set a new YTD record on purchases from central banks across the world. This is still below the November 2024 spike, which I believe was a response to the U.S. election.

But central banks are not speculators, and they are often more concerned with how much gold they have on hand than how much it's worth. This is less true for the new player on the scene, which had been buying gold before now but recently ratcheted up their efforts: cryptocurrency issuers.

Securing Stablecoins With Gold

Those in the cryptocurrency space will likely recognize the ticker USDT-USD, often just called "Tether." It's named after the company that issues it, and it is the world's largest stablecoin. These are cryptocurrencies chained to the value of a dollar. One USDT should always carry the same value as one dollar, and since we price the asset in dollars (or at least I do as an American), that should make the currency interchangeable with real dollars. Crypto skeptics will argue otherwise, but that is for another article.

When I first heard reports of Tether buying gold, this made a lot of sense to me. They also issue a cryptocurrency tied to the price of gold, XAUT-USD, and as they issue more of that coin, they need to buy the requisite amount of gold to back up the value.

But now, Tether has more gold on hand than the Shanghai Futures Exchange. This is a huge shift, and it happened quickly.

I knew that USDT was not entirely backed by dollars. Instead, it's backed by a mix of reserves, mostly dollars and deposits, but also Bitcoin and gold ("precious metals"). What's interesting is that this rush for gold wasn't just going to back XAUT issuance; most of it went to USDT's balance sheet.

From Q2 to Q3 this year, they increased their precious metals holdings on USDT's balance sheet from $8 billion to nearly $13 billion. That's a big jump in just one quarter.

Here are their current assets as of the last quarterly report:

When we compare this to central bank demand, we're starting to see Tether alone take up more and more of the total demand. Keep in mind, the "central banks" bar is all the world's central banks combined, including the PBOC.

What's most fascinating is when you see Tether compared to these central banks individually. It really shows how staggering this recent accumulation has been. This is just for Q3 this year.

It's Unlikely That Others Will Follow

Here's the tricky part. Immediately, I think there will be many people who jump to -- well, if Tether's doing this, then demand for gold from other stablecoin issuers like the second-largest USDC-USD, issued by Circle (CRCL) should follow.

There's a wrench in the plans. One named "GENIUS." Passed earlier this year, the Genius Act requires that U.S.-based stablecoin issuers have full 1:1 reserves backing the stablecoins they issue, and these reserves can only be held in deposits and in Treasuries.

Here is the relevant text from the bill:

(1) In general.—A permitted payment stablecoin issuer shall—

(A) maintain identifiable reserves backing the outstanding payment stablecoins of the permitted payment stablecoin issuer on an at least 1 to 1 basis, with reserves comprising—

(i) United States coins and currency (including Federal Reserve notes) or money standing to the credit of an account with a Federal Reserve Bank;

(ii) funds held as demand deposits (or other deposits that may be withdrawn upon request at any time) or insured shares at an insured depository institution (including any foreign branches or agents, including correspondent banks, of an insured depository institution), subject to limitations established by the Corporation and the National Credit Union Administration, as applicable, to address safety and soundness risks of such insured depository institution;

(iii) Treasury bills, notes, or bonds—

(I) with a remaining maturity of 93 days or less; or

(II) issued with a maturity of 93 days or less;

(iv) money received under repurchase agreements, with the permitted payment stablecoin issuer acting as a seller of securities and with an overnight maturity, that are backed by Treasury bills with a maturity of 93 days or less;

(V) reverse repurchase agreements, with the permitted payment stablecoin issuer acting as a purchaser of securities and with an overnight maturity, that are collateralized by Treasury notes, bills, or bonds on an overnight basis, subject to overcollateralization in line with standard market terms...

This means that U.S.-based issuers will be unable to hold any gold on their balance sheet. Could they issue gold-backed coins like XAUT-USD? Absolutely. But this demand may be isolated to Tether since they are the only large stablecoin issuer outside the United States.

That being said, Tether makes up 60% of the stablecoin market globally. So even though other issuers won't follow this change and the strategic shift from Tether is very bullish for gold, I don't see a world in which the purchases slow down any time soon given how stablecoins are becoming more and more popular.

BRICS Currency Speculation

All of this doesn't account for the fact that I believe in the next five years we will see a BRICS-centric payment system that relies on a basket of precious metals (of which gold will likely dominate).

For the record, BRICS is an informal economic alliance between Brazil, Russia, India, China, South Africa, and the economies aligned to them ("BRICS+").

Before I get any comments of you're crazy, nothing can ever replace the dollar, I doubt that a BRICS currency would actually be aimed as a global dollar replacement but as a means of dollar avoidance by the specific economies in question.

I don't even think it would be used in the countries that are part of BRICS. A BRICS currency would not be designed to replace the Renminbi, Rupee, Real, Ruble, Rand, etc. (first time I've realized that all the BRICS currencies start with R), from my view. Instead, it would be used as a medium between these countries and likely between oil producers in OPEC.

To get the Saudis and Emirates to de-dollarize, something that China has long wanted, they would need something that is as trusted as the dollar, since the BRICS currencies mentioned above are not as trustworthy as the dollar due to devaluation and authoritarian practices in the governments in regard to fiat currencies.

A currency backed by a basket of precious metals and redeemable for those precious metals at the central banks, like the PBOC, could make a lot of sense.

There just isn't enough gold for it to be entirely gold-backed. But if we added silver, platinum, and palladium to the mix, things start to check out a little more. Look at where these metals are mined. Of special note is the monopoly BRICS has on platinum and palladium mining.

Like I said, I don't think this is a replacement for domestic currencies, but rather it will be mostly digitally transferred as a means of having a hard currency that can pay for oil, which is currently almost completely dollarized.

U.S. sanctions on Lukoil and Rosneft only complicate this because they're forcing China and India away from Russian oil and into the dollarized trade coming out of the Middle East. Over time, I expect the dollar paradigm to be challenged, and that will be a significant catalyst for the prices of most precious metals, gold in particular.

This is not something that will happen immediately, but slowly and quietly. The indicator to me that it has begun is the rise in central bank purchases in the first place, especially the PBOC, which ramped up gold purchases right after we weaponized the dollar and froze $300 billion in Russian assets following their invasion of Ukraine.

How I'm Buying Gold Exposure

I am still very bullish on gold, mostly for the reasons I've outlined here and in my last report. The structural shifts in demand are not coming from the United States, and U.S. institutions still just look like speculators. This is changing quickly, and shifts like that of Tether are very indicative that not only are central banks hoarding gold, but now so are cryptocurrency issuers. That opens up a whole new lane for gold demand.

Currently, my largest gold-related holding has become GDMN, which I covered last month. It is a fund that owns both gold and global gold miners. This is what I recommend to most people because it allows you to take a smaller stance and get more exposure to both gold and the miners that dig it up, which often can rise in value faster than gold itself during these bullish cycles.

Conclusion

I see the structural demand for gold as growing, and this includes not only central banks but now also another kind of central bank: cryptocurrency issuers. Gold is being bought up as part of a means of backing USDT, which makes up 60% of the stablecoin market. This is a huge shift, and one that shouldn't be ignored.

Of course, speculation is still rampant, and gold's recent volatility has proven that while U.S. investors are not accumulating and allocating to gold like those in the rest of the world, they are still trading it heavily. I expect that volatility to continue moving forward, but that is not necessarily a bad thing. It does warrant caution.

As always, and especially with gold now, this line is prescient:

Position sizing is often more important than security selection.

Thanks for reading.

Editor's Note: This article covers one or more microcap stocks. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Alimentation Couche-Tard: Timeless Upside From Food, But Uncertain At $74/Share
data.description:
Note: Unless noted, dollar amounts are in CAD.

So, Alimentation Couche-Tard (ATD:CA), or simply Couche-Tard is a Canadian multinational food (among other things) retailer. For those of you who are familiar with my work, you know that I've, over the years, spent a lot of time and capital investing in and following up on food companies. Not only in Sweden or the Nordics, which is my main "home", but also in areas like Eastern Europe, Portugal, Brazil, the USA, to now entering Canada, and look at this company.

This company operates a lot of convenience stores and gas stations under several brands. The most noteworthy of the brands that the company owns is not, in my perspective, even Couche-tard, but Circle K. Circle K is one of the foremost fuel stations and chains in the entire world, and it's also the brand that the company uses most often outside of Canada.

Aside from Circle K, we're looking at brands like Holiday Superstores, like Couche-Tard, like Ingo (fuel, but unmanned), Irving Oil, and others. The company includes both franchises (like "On the Run") as well as its wholly owned brands.

ATD has been on a mission to simplify its brands, which is why most of the fuel stations on an international basis have been replaced with Circle K branding. In the Nordics, this was one of the things they did, when they agreed to buy the Norwegian Statoil Fuel and Retail for almost $3B. This gave the company market leadership in the Scandinavian and somewhat Baltic fuel market.

So, Alimentation Couche-Tard is a multinational leader in fuel (and lately also charging, because the company's fuel stations are obviously adding this as well), as well as groceries in key areas.

In this article, I will try to determine if this company is a good idea to invest in during a market that's seeing what I consider to be significant overvaluation.

What appeals to me in the company's fundamentals is that the company is clearly preparing to, and has already entered, what I consider to be growth markets in both ME, Asia, and Africa.

So let's see what we have here.

Alimentation Couche-Tard - Upside from operations across the world

It's fair to call the company more of a convenience chain than a grocery store, even if the company does sell grocery items. Much of its sales are done in conjunction however, with fuel and convenience, and that's where most of its operations are found.

The company generates annual revenues justifying a market capitalization of nearly $70B(CAD), with a rating of BBB+ in terms of credit. What drew me to ATD, aside from these operational specifics you see above, is the company's forecast accuracy. More than 30-55% of the time, depending on if you look at the 1-year or 2-year basis, ATD beats targets and estimates. Growth for this company is forecasted at over 13% per year over the next 3-4 years, and together with an admittedly low dividend of 1.16% at today's price, this makes the company interesting at the right price.

The company's market opportunities for this significant growth forecast lies in merging fragmented markets, similar to how it has done in Scandinavia and Sweden. With Circle K and Ingo, the only real competition offered the company is now 1-2 major chains. Smaller fuel and petrol stations here are rare. So market fragmentation here is low - but this is not the case in say, the US.

ATD is focusing on this fragmented market for further and future upside.

ATD also has a proven history of being able to grow both sales and EBIT significantly over time. Its method of doing so has been inorganic growth, meaning M&A's and additions. Over the past 15-20 years, sales for the company have grown for over 15 consecutive years.

The company has a nearly 50-year history, and its current assets encompass over 17,000 convenience stores across the world. Most of the company's sales, aside from straight fuel, are tobacco products and fresh grocery items. As mentioned, an important part of its expansion that it has been pushing here in Scandinavia is its renewable charging network, and it has opened some of its first EV-only charging hubs.

It's fair to say that the company is "in the time", and regardless of which way the EV automotive revolution goes, the company will not fail because it has failed to prepare for e-mobility. Current targets are over 1,000 ultra-fast charging points within the next 13 months.

In the US, competition for Couche-tard is primarily Murphy (MUSA) and Casey's (CASY). I will be looking at those investment alternatives in the valuation section.

The problems with Couche-tard are primarily related to its inorganic growth focus. The company has been part of several failed or bad deals and M&A's, and this also remains the single most destructive potential on a forward basis. The latest target for ATD was 7-Eleven, and thankfully, the company backed off from this deal, largely to avoid dilution. This was in July of this year. Carrefour (OTCPK:CRARY) was also stopped by the French government (which I am happy about, being a Carrefour shareholder and believing the company worth far more), as well as a failed Speedway Deal, though this was now 4 years ago.

But these risks are not to be overstated. The company is, after all, not just around any longer, but is also thriving, and has been able to successfully integrate large brands and chains into its operations. I would characterize the company as a skilled capital allocator, though one that needs to be viewed with a close eye for shareholder dilution and value destruction. Also, I view it as less of a risk these days, because as big as the company is now (and with the declines that we've seen), the remaining targets for constructive M&A's are relatively few. Together with successful strategies such as private brands, as seen above, or expanding food, I see potential for upside, which I obviously am not the only one to see, given the company's forecasted upside.

As for the latest results, they essentially confirm the company's upside. Strong earnings growth of 5.4%, growth in merchandise and service returns, and a significant increase in net earnings. Canada saw most of that growth, with European macro being reflective of poor/slow growth. If you consider the company to be interesting, the biggest things to watch out for would, in my view, be home-market (Canadian, US) margins. The big changes able to be driven by the company is a more efficient handling of its selection. it requires the further (and larger use) of private labels in the company's stores, the reduction of other labels and products. Much of the margin improvements come from that - and if the company manages to continue on this road, there's a meaningful margin improvement possible, even if at low sales increases. This also concerns distribution efficiency.

It's worth at least mentioning that US sales trends differ wildly across states. Some are up over 5%, but some major areas for company sales mix are negative in sales (like Texas and Arizona, together over 22% of merchandise sales). So it's worth mentioning that volatility should be accounted for.

With good results out of the way, let's look at the valuation for the company here.

Alimentation Couche-Tard valuation - almost impossible to get cheap

This isn't the first or even the fourth time I've looked at ATD. Over time, it has become clear to me that getting this company at an undervaluation is very tricky. It's not currently undervalued either, as I see it. A significant dip in earnings was recorded last year and in the last fiscal year, which is fairly unique for the business. But current forecasts are back to growth, with a 20-year average P/E of 18x.

Unfortunately, this is where we run into issues. Even at the high expectation for ATD, even with a 13%+ annualized EPS growth, and even allowing for that 18x P/E, the current upside for the company is less than 13% per year. Part of the culprit is the dividend, but the fact remains that this company is not currently cheap.

Even with a high upside like that, you're not getting what I generally would look for. When looking at alternatives, as in alternative investments, there's Casey's, which shares some of ATD's appeal in terms of forecast beating, but is also significantly overvalued at almost twice that of ATD. The company also has a marginal yield of less than 0.5% per share, which makes it even less attractive at this price. The other alternative is Murphy US, and this is probably the cheapest of the lot. However, MUSA also comes with problems. Less growth at below 9% per year, a BB+ junk-rated credit as opposed to ATD's BBB+, and a market capitalization that is less than a third of that of ATD. With that comparison, even the lower valuation and potential upside are not enough to make me interested in an investment there.

I would characterize the sector as one to keep a very close eye on in the event of a downturn, but would not invest in ATD or any of its peers at this time.

In terms of introductory price, I give ATD a fair value of 18.8x forward P/E, of about $97/share, which means that my PT for the company comes to about $68/share for a clear, 15% annualized upside even in the case of less positive returns than expected.

Risks & Considerations

The primary risks to this company with my current rating is the failure of inorganic growth as well as the macro. This is one of those companies where other risks, as I see them, are few and far between. Because of its sheer size, the cash generation it has, ATD is able to weather most any storm or downturn. With its expansion into charging, the e-mobility argument against the company does not work either. It's already in a good position to be able to manage this.

There is the possibility that in a shift in mobility, declining fuel sales could hurt ATD appeal and returns as they normalize and find their new levels, with a slight drop off with people being able to "fuel" their cars at home through electric. But I believe there will be an organic evolution here, and that the shift will not be as excessive as some seem to believe.

Based on this, ATD has the following thesis for me.

Thesis

While this company represents a very solid potential holding in a conservative portfolio, combining all the best parts of fuel and convenience store sales, the upside is only good enough if you buy it at a cheap enough price, and if you're forecasting it at a reasonable forward P/E.

At the current valuation, my preference is to "wait". The company's 18.8x P/E is too high for the average, and for the upside, even with double-digit growth. It would require over 19-20x P/E for a 15% annualized rate of return, and I don't see that in the company's cards here.

For that reason, and not due to fundamental appeal, I consider ATD to be a "HOLD" here, and would wait for a share price of below $68/share.

Here are my criteria and how the company fulfills them (in italics).

  • This company is overall qualitative.
  • This company is fundamentally safe/conservative & well-run.
  • This company pays a well-covered dividend.
  • This company is currently cheap.
  • This company has a realistic upside that is high enough, based on earnings growth or multiple expansion/reversion.

The company fulfills 3 out of 3 quality indicators, and 0 out of 2 valuation indicators. That means 3/5 "stars" here, which typically isn't enough. Based on this, and a valuation target of $68/share where I see a risk/reward-adjusted upside that's attractive enough for me. I give the company a "Hold" rating as of December 2025.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Dollar Tree's Halloween Sales Were Scary Good, But There's A Twist
data.description:
Dollar Tree's Q3 2025 Earnings Review

Retailer earnings week has started, and Dollar Tree (DLTR) is up 4% on the day of its earnings release.

Last quarter, we saw the company dismissing its Family Dollar business for around $1B to focus on its core discount business. Now, we find it reporting strong momentum and a record-breaking Halloween season. Sales reached $4.7B, up 9.4% YoY, with same-store sales up by 4.2% (avg. ticket up 4.5%, traffic down 0.3%). Even with the impact of tariffs, DLTR was able to increase its gross margin by 40 bps to 35.8%. The report was a beat both on the top and the bottom line, with EPS of $1.21 coming in $0.13 above the consensus.

If we zoom out and look at the first nine months of the year, things are going well. Net sales are up 11% to $13.9B, with same store sales up 5.4% (1.8% traffic; 3.6% average ticket).

Moreover, the company disclosed its Q4 outlook, expecting comparable sales to grow between 4% and 6%. This means FY sales should come just shy of $19.5B while FY EPS should be around $5.70, reflecting the favorable impact of share repurchases (YtD: $1.31B for 15M shares; with the addition of another 1.7M shares bought after the end of Q3 for $176M).

When we look at the trends that Dollar Tree discloses regarding how its comparable sales have developed since 2022, we see that Q3 was the first quarter since Q4 2022 with negative traffic. At the same time, Dollar Tree is now on a 5-quarter streak of higher average ticket, where we see favorable momentum as we get ready to close 2025 and head into 2026.

This is confirmed by a number Dollar Tree disclosed during the earnings call: 3M more households shopped at its stores in Q3 2025 versus Q3 2024. Interestingly, we heard that:

Approximately 60% of these incremental shoppers came from higher-income households, those earning over $100,000, 30% from middle-income households, those earning between $60,000 to $100,000 with the rest from lower-income households, those earning under $60,000. Importantly, Q3 spending growth was broad-based across all income subcohorts, including households earning below $20,000.

Surely, a big part of Dollar Tree's current success comes from its pivot to a multi-price strategy that the company adopted back in 2019, and that is now attracting more and more customers. Dollar Tree management took as an example its Halloween sales and compared its 2022 mix versus this year's:

To see the full impact of multi-price, let's go back to Halloween 2022, when multi-price was still in its infancy. That year, multi-price represented about 3% of units sold, 10% of sales, and 7% of merchandise gross margin across our full Halloween assortment. Fast forward to 2025 on a 25% larger base of sales, where multi-price accounted for roughly 1/4 of our total Halloween sales and merchandise gross margin, but only 8% of Halloween units sold.

Moreover, we learned that every multi-price item generates 3.5x more profits compared to the non-multi-price items that the company sells. In hindsight, we must acknowledge that Dollar Tree's strategy is paying off.

Below, we see that both DLTR and its main competitor, Dollar General (DG), have been experiencing sustained foot traffic. We have discussed this every time we have dealt with retailers this year: we are seeing lower-income consumers becoming cost-conscious and adopting trade-down behaviors, seeking value, and reducing the volume of their purchases. In this environment, we have heard people talking about inflation fatigue and an economy at two speeds. Probably, had it not been for the outbreak of AI, the economy might have experienced a recession, but currently, the U.S. GDP is pushed up by the largest investment cycle the world has ever seen.

Dollar Tree's Valuation

There is no doubt that the company is seeing favorable momentum and that its execution has enabled it to grow at a faster pace compared to many of its peers. The current environment, with inflation still pressuring many consumers, is perfect for the company to thrive and expand. But Dollar Tree's success doesn't rely only on favorable tailwinds; it also comes from some choices that its management took to focus more and more on the core value proposition.

So, is Dollar Tree a buy right now? Well, it currently trades at a fwd PE of 19.2, which is below the market average. Its fwd EV/EBITDA is 12.7, which is a bit expensive as the sector median is 10.4. However, this is not an exuberant multiple, and it is more or less in line with Dollar General's multiples. Dollar Tree has outperformed its main peer this year, and this makes sense since its execution has been impeccable. Today, DLTR is not, therefore, a screaming bargain, trading more or less around fair value. I would not give Dollar Tree an earnings multiple in the low to mid-20s. After all, there are some risks, as consumer spending sensitivity is something this business always has to face. In this case, the risk is not a recession, but a rebound. If consumers feel less inflationary pressure, higher-income households could go back to their usual retailers. All in all, while DLTR keeps generating stable EBITDA and cash flows, the fair EV/EBITDA multiple stays between 11 and 13. So, I would not be aggressive in buying the stock right now. If you already own it, there is no reason to sell, as you are probably sitting on some nice gains. But for people who have not bought the stock in previous years, this is not the right moment to buy it. Therefore, I stick to my neutral rating.

data.title: IJH: Core Mid-Cap Equities As A Foundation For A Diversified Portfolio Strategy
data.description:
The iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF (IJH) is a passively managed, low-cost exchange-traded fund designed to provide investors with broad exposure to US mid-cap equities. The strategy is widely adopted as a core equity strategy with over $100b in net assets and provides substantial trading depth for active portfolio rotation. IJH can be utilized by investors as the foundation for building a portfolio, actively rotating out of IJH into satellite positions as new investment opportunities arise.

About iShares Core S&P Mid-Cap ETF

IJH was launched by iShares on May 22, 2000 on the NYSE Arca Exchange. IJH has a low expense ratio of 5bps and pays out a modest distribution rate of $0.83/share with a 30-day SEC yield of 1.21%. IJH is benchmarked to the S&P Mid-Cap 400 Index as provided by S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC.

The Index largely covers US mid-cap equities with a market capitalization of $7.4-$20.5b at the time of inclusion. The Index weighs constituents based on market capitalization, meaning that larger mid-cap companies will have a bigger influence on the Index when compared to smaller companies. For example, the top 10 holdings account for 7.65% of the total portfolio weight whereas the bottom 10 holdings make up 0.43% of net assets. Though the top holdings make up a larger weight, the level of concentration risk is relatively minimal, particularly when compared to large-cap indices like the S&P 500 (SPX) where the top 10 constituents account for 39.51% of the portfolio weight. Given that the top holding in IJH, Comfort Systems USA (FIX), has a portfolio weight of roughly 1.07%, I do not believe any single holding will have substantial influence over the performance of the portfolio.

IJH is heavily weighted in industrials, making up 23.72% of the portfolio weight. Other large sector weightings include financials at 16.38%, information technology at 13.69%, and consumer discretionary at 11.65%. By comparison, the S&P 500 has a sector concentration of 35.29% in technology, 12.79% in financials, and 10.98% in communications. Given the sector weights, I believe IJH provides substantially more sector diversification while limiting exposure to the top constituents.

Looking at peer mid-cap strategies, investors may consider the State Street SPDR Portfolio S&P 400 Mid Cap ETF (SPMD) or the Vanguard Mid Cap Index Fund ETF (VO) as alternatives to IJH, each offering a slightly lower expense ratio with a relatively similar strategy.

VO will be a more concentrated investment strategy with 293 individual holdings, far below IJH and SPMD. Given that we’re considering IJH as a core position, I believe diversification will be a critical factor for fund selection as equity or sector concentration can be added through additional investment strategies.

Investor Suitability

IJH was designed to be used as a core mid-cap strategy, meaning that IJH can be used as a foundational position in a portfolio with satellite positions added to adjust equity or sector concentration. Unlike the S&P 500, IJH will provide investors with a larger concentration in the industrials sector which may be appealing for investors seeking non-AI-related investments.

Though IJH pays out a quarterly distribution rate annualized at $0.83/share, I do not believe the 1.26% yield necessarily makes the portfolio strategy appealing from an income perspective.

I believe the best way to utilize IJH is to position one’s equity strategy around IJH. From a tactical perspective, investors may utilize IJH as a placeholder for unallocated funds in their equity portfolio, rotating funds from IJH to other ETFs or equities to gain exposure to other markets that may be underrepresented in IJH. Taking into consideration sector weightings, investors may consider adding energy or communications exposure by adding an ETF or equity investments.

IJH can also be used for macroeconomic traders that may utilize a more thematic approach to making investment decisions. For example, mid-cap equities may outperform large-caps during an interest rate reduction cycle as these companies, particularly those in the industrial sector, may be more sensitive to the cost of financing debt.

IJH can be used for both long-term and short-term investment horizons. IJH has roughly $100b in net assets with an average of $657mm in share value changing hands on a daily basis, providing investors with substantial liquidity and trading depth for actively managing a position.

IJH is a passively managed strategy designed to provide investors with exposure to the S&P Mid-Cap 400 Index. Given that this is a passively managed strategy, investors will be responsible for tactical positioning as the fund will not adjust the strategy based on market conditions. Sector concentration may limit growth potential for IJH given the smaller allocation to the technology sector.

Final Thoughts

IJH can be an appealing core mid-cap equity holding for investors seeking to actively manage a portfolio strategy. IJH can best be utilized as a foundation or a placeholder for capital as an investor builds a portfolio of satellite positions around the core equity holding.

This article answers these three main questions about IJH:

  • Does IJH offer diversification away from large-cap tech names that dominate the broader indices?
  • Is IJH actively managed or passively managed?
  • What type of investor is IJH most suitable for?

Editor's note: This article is intended to provide a general overview of the ETF for educational purposes only and, unlike other articles on Seeking Alpha, does not offer an investment opinion about the ETF.

data.title: TIC Solutions: Upside Is Visible, But The Company Needs To Prove Itself
data.description:
TIC Solutions (TIC) is a service company dedicated to testing, inspection and certification services and engineering. In its current form, TIC is a combination of two companies, Acuren and NV5 Global, and their integration has just begun. TIC Solutions is focused on energy, infrastructure and industrial end markets.

TIC Solutions reminds me of an investment case on Atlas Technical Consultants, which I published in 2022. Atlas Technical Consultants was taken public as a SPAC and it had a little messy financials, high debt and a series of acquisitions behind it. Like TIC Solutions, Atlas was engaged in testing, inspection and certification and technical design. In 2023, the company was acquired by a private equity firm which paid 124% premium and EV/EBITDA multiple of 11.5x.

Also Acuren was listed through a SPAC in 2025. Similarly to Atlas, many of its services are mandatory to its customers due to regulation and compliance, therefore a large part of its revenue is sticky and recurring. As a service provider, its business does not tie up capital and delivers strong cash flow. Both companies have a strong track record of acquisitive growth and resilience over cycles. The early results of the integration of the two companies are promising and the shares have upside if the company can meet the higher end of its guidance.

More diversified combination of two equals

TIC Solutions has three main segments, Inspection & Mitigation, Consulting Engineering and Geospatial. Most of its $2 billion revenue comes from the U.S., primarily from customers in infrastructure, energy and industrial sectors. One fifth of the business originates from the public-sector. TIC Solutions has 11 000 employees and over 250 locations. EY Parthenon estimates the global testing, inspection and certification market to grow 4-5% annually.

In 2024, NV5 posted a revenue of approximately $1 billion, so the merger is a combination of almost equal sized companies. Acuren’s main service lines included non-destructive testing, rope access and engineering and laboratory testing, whereas NV5’s focus was on consulting engineering and geospatial services. Now, the new entity is more diversified in terms of end markets and services although NV5’s margins were lower than Acuren’s.

In the first nine months, TIC Solutions grew revenue by 5%. The third quarter came in slower, proforma revenue growth of 2.4%. In Q3, adjusted EBITDA margin declined by 0.6 %-points to 16.3% but was higher than YTD margin of 15.4%. On top of the merger, TIC Solutions has made four bolt-on acquisitions this year, which support future growth.

The company is in a show-me situation

In the merger the owners of NV5 received cash and shares worth $23 per share, a 32% premium. Including the debt, the deal resulted in an enterprise value of $1.7 billion. The price paid equaled EV/EBITDA-multiple of approximately 12x. In 2024, NV5 grew revenue by 10% mainly thanks to acquisitions and adjusted EBITDA grew by 7%. It had a modest leverage ratio of 1.4x.

NV5 brought in Consulting Engineering and Geospatial revenue. However before the deal it pursued to highlight its testing, inspection and certification offering. Although NV5 did not disclose its revenue split by activity, this is most likely one of the main motivations for the merger. Now, NV5 revenue is grouped into two slices, Consulting Engineering and Geospatial, of the pie chart above describing the revenue split between the segments.

NV5’s businesses take the combined company closer to a couple of growth opportunities. Naturally, datacenters are one of them. Its sales to the datacenter sector “more than doubled” over the last 12 months. The sales to datacenters is only 3% of total revenue, but so far it is delivering only a limited amount of services to the sector, according to the Q3 earnings call. Expanding the offering should provide a long growth runway in the vertical.

TIC has highlighted the opportunities for cross-selling as a justification for the merger as TIC pursues to sell more services to the clients of each company. It has presented compelling case stories, however investors should remain cautious. Customers could already have their trusted partners in both lines of businesses and changing the service provider might be more difficult and take longer than initially expected.

TIC Solutions has launched some new services created by a combination of assets and expertise from the two merged companies. One such opportunity is expanding NV5’s services to Canada, where Acuren generated 43% of its revenue. Surprisingly fast TIC has come up and implemented entirely new offerings that combine NV5’s capabilities and Acuren’s field force.

Integration of Acuren and NV5 has just begun. Apparently, the early results are promising. TIC raised its near-term cost synergy target from $20 million to $25 million. Also, the company expects to achieve synergies faster than originally communicated.

Traditionally most of the testing, inspection and certification firms, such as Eurofins, Intertek, UL Solution and Bureau Veritas, are pure plays. Now, many investors might be confused by the Consulting Engineering segment of TIC. The management needs to do better work communicating what it includes as benchmarking and peer valuation could appear more problematic. It is good to remember that the above mentioned peers have greater focus on products, especially going into the consumer market. TIC Solutions is more focused on more industrial customers and utilities, which require field work for instance.

High debt and negative earnings might repel some investors

The merger and high debt led TIC Solutions to issue new shares through a private placement to an existing shareholder. In October, it issued 20.8 million shares worth $250 million at a price of $12 per share. In other words, the dilution was approximately 10%. On the other hand, share issuance helped the company with its debt position. In the future, TIC should be able to de-lever thanks to its good cash flow generation.

The acquisition of NV5 increased TIC’s leverage ratio to 4.4x, while the target ratio is 3x. It has long-term debt of $1.6 billion without maturities in the near future. Due to significant interest expense depreciation and amortization and transaction costs, TIC Solutions reported negative earnings for the first nine months. As long as TIC reports negative earnings the stock remains in the shadows to many investors.

Fairly valued but upside is found in an optimistic scenario

The management has given certain parameters for 2026. They expect revenue growth of 3% to 5% and adjusted EBITDA-margin of 15.5% to 16.5%. It would mean revenue of approximately $2.17 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $330 million, calculated by lower range figures. This would mean EV/EBITDA multiple of 11x. These figures are for the combined entity as 2026 will be a year with full NV5 impact. For 2026, analysts are expecting revenue of $2.21 billion so in order to meet the estimates TIC needs to deliver figures closer to the upper end of its guidance.

If we play with the most optimistic figures of management’s guidance, significant upside emerges. If TIC Solutions meets the upper end of its revenue ($2.15 billion) guidance this year, delivers 5% revenue growth in 2026 and reaches 16.5% EBITDA-margin, the share price would reach $11.7, assuming $1.5 billion net debt, no change in the multiple or the number of shares outstanding. Assuming EV/EBITDA-multiple would increase to 12x, share price would be $13.3.

On a forward EV/EBITDA basis TIC Solutions trades in line with the other testing, inspection and certification peers according to Koyfin. The group’s average EV/EBITDA multiple stands exactly at 10.3x according to Koyfin. The French Eurofins has the lowest ratio of 8.3x and UL Solutions the highest, 20.3x. Is TIC Solutions as good business as the average? Based on EBITDA-margin it is not. TIC Solutions should improve its margin by 4-5 %-points to meet the level of its peers. In addition, when looking at the acquisition multiples of NV5, previously mentioned Atlas Technical Consultants or Spanish Applus, it is difficult to see large potential for multiple expansion.

Lately, analyst following has increased from two to four. The target prices range from $11.5 to $17 averaging a little below $14.

Conclusion

TIC Solutions operates in attractive and sticky business lines. For many investors the investment case could be in a wait-and-see mode as the merger proceeds. At the moment, TIC Solutions appears neutrally valued. The potential upside relies on better than expected sales and cost synergies. A rather unique combination needs to prove its value. In a scenario where the company delivers market growth, improves slightly its profitability and perhaps pays down debt, investors can achieve a significant price appreciation.

data.title: Datadog: Multiproduct Sales And AI Deals Underpin Strong Growth (Upgrade)
data.description:
As we approach the end of the year, we look back at a very volatile 2025 that has seen a lot of upheaval in the stock markets, especially as the growth stock category experienced a change of leadership. In particular, many of the enterprise software stocks that led COVID-era gains have pulled back and underperformed the S&P 500, despite having AI tailwinds of their own.

Datadog (DDOG), the infrastructure monitoring software stock, is up ~10% this year, underperforming the S&P 500 but well ahead of many battered enterprise software peers like HubSpot (HUBS) and Atlassian (TEAM), where AI is considered to be a bigger threat. Amid strong recent results for Datadog, however, we think the company is on the cusp of regaining its momentum.

I last wrote an article upgrading Datadog to a "Neutral" rating in September, when the stock was trading around $140. Recently, on Nov. 6, Datadog reported a strong Q3 beat-and-raise, which immediately prompted a 20%+ rally to ~$200: gains that were quickly reversed, even though the clear demand tailwinds that Datadog is showcasing appear to be well sustained. I view the volatile pullback as a great opportunity to buy Datadog as it looks to push higher, and I'm upgrading the stock to a "Buy" rating.

For investors who are newer to Datadog, here is what I consider to be the long-term bull case for this stock:

  • Clear land and expand momentum. Unlike subscription-based software companies, Datadog's revenue ultimately depends on customers' consumption of its products. Despite clear IT budget moderation this year, Datadog has boosted its net revenue retention rates to ~120%, reflecting an average customer who boosts its spending by ~20%.
  • Multiproduct sales and tailwinds from AI and non-AI customers. Deal momentum from AI companies remains a core reason that Datadog can sustain near-30% revenue growth despite its >$3 billion annualized revenue scale, but the company notes that standard non-AI customers are also upgrading their packages and purchasing multiple products on the Datadog platform.
  • Rule of 50. Forget the "Rule of 40" - Datadog combines a mid-20s operating margin with a high-20s revenue growth rate, putting Datadog's Rule of 40 score above 50.
  • Growing cloud security opportunity. More and more, customers are turning to Datadog's monitoring capabilities as a security solution. The company believes the TAM for cloud security is growing at a 17% CAGR, which is double the 9% expected CAGR in observability, while Datadog's near-30% revenue growth (eclipsing growth in both markets) suggests that Datadog is taking share.

Meanwhile, there are two main risks to Datadog that I'm tracking closely. The first is that Datadog is investing heavily into sales and marketing to capture as much business as it can in the AI age, which is reflected in a slight y/y decay in operating margins (which I think is more than justified, as Datadog has managed to maintain healthy growth rates and its Rule of 50 positioning).

The second is a premium valuation. At current share prices near $157, Datadog trades at a market cap of $53.96 billion. Netting off the $4.14 billion of cash and $982.4 million of convertible debt on Datadog's most recent balance sheet gives us an enterprise value of $50.80 billion.

For the remainder of FY25, Datadog has substantially boosted its full-year revenue outlook to $3.385-$3.39 billion, or 26% y/y growth, versus 24% y/y growth expected previously. Its operating margin guidance, meanwhile, now reflects a 22% full-year margin.

For FY26, Street estimates point to $4.10 billion in revenue for Datadog, reflecting 21% y/y growth. This positions Datadog's valuation multiples at:

  • 15.1x EV/FY25 revenue
  • 12.4x EV/FY26 revenue

I now consider Datadog to be in the "middle of the pack" when it comes to mid-20s growth software companies. There are penalty-box stocks like Atlassian (TEAM) and GitLab (GTLB) that are growing at a similar pace and yet trade at much cheaper multiples (due to the prevalent market narrative that vibe coding will take away software developer jobs that are the main paid seats on these platforms). That said, there are also still stocks like ServiceNow (NOW) and JFrog (FROG) that trade in line with Datadog:

Considering Datadog's ability to showcase strong high-20s growth without any deceleration in a tough budget environment, plus deliver improvements in net retention rate, I think Datadog still leaves room for further upside. Stay long here.

Q3 download

Let's now go through Datadog's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q3 earnings summary is shown below:

Datadog's revenue grew 28% y/y to $885.7 million, beating Wall Street's expectations of $851.3 million (+23% y/y) by a huge five-point margin. We note as well that Datadog's revenue growth didn't decelerate at all versus 23% y/y growth in Q2. The company has demonstrated a very conservative slant in its guidance: its latest outlook calls for growth to fall to ~24% in Q4 as well, but given Datadog's own history, we expect the company to outperform.

Catalyzing strong growth for the company is the growing trend for customers to adopt multiple products on the Datadog platform, especially its cloud security solutions. The share of customers that are using more than 8 products at Datadog has grown to 16% of its total customer base, 4 points higher y/y. Meanwhile, customers using 6+ products has grown to 31% of the customer base, up 7 points from 24% of customers last year.

Perhaps most meaningful is the fact that after Datadog saw net retention rates slide to the ~mid 110s last year, the company has clocked in at a ~120% net retention rate over the past two quarters. We note that many enterprise software companies have noted difficulties with net retention this year amid budget caution. HubSpot (HUBS), for example, is barely showcasing any net expansion. Datadog's improvement in net retention despite a tough macro climate showcases that the company's investments into sales are paying off.

Per CEO Olivier Pomel's remarks on the Q3 earnings call regarding go-to-market momentum, which was spread across both AI companies and non-AI business:

We have seen broad-based positive trends in the demand environment with an ongoing strength of cloud migration and digital transformation. Against this backdrop, we executed a very strong Q3 both in new logo bookings and usage growth of existing customers.

As a notable inflection, we saw acceleration of year-over-year revenue growth across our non-AI customers. And the sequential usage growth for non-AI existing customers was the highest we have seen going back 12 quarters. This growth was broad-based as our customers are adopting more products and getting more value from the Datadog platform.

We also experienced strong revenue growth for our AI native customers and a broadening contribution to growth among those customers. There, too, we saw an acceleration of growth in our AI cohort in Q3 when excluding our largest customer.

Looking at new business. Contribution from new customers increased in Q3 in both the amount of new customer bookings as well as the revenue contribution from new customers. And as usual, churn has remained low with gross revenue retention stable in the mid- to high 90s, highlighting the mission-critical nature of our platform for our customers.

That said, we are mindful of the fact that the company has stepped up its sales spending, with sales and marketing costs growing 25% y/y to $199.5 million, excluding stock comp. R&D spending is also jumped 38% y/y to $269.5 million.

As a result, Datadog's pro forma operating margins pulled back 2 points y/y to 23%, which still puts Datadog firmly in the "Rule of 50" camp. We note that the company's long-term operating model calls for pro forma operating margins to stabilize above 25%, though we note that the company's current levels of cash flow and profitability aren't yet enough to justify the stock's valuation and move it off a revenue multiples-based assessment.

Key takeaways

With investors chasing the hottest AI stocks in the 2025 stock market, many buying opportunities have actually opened up in former Wall Street darlings that were too expensive to buy during the COVID era. Datadog has underperformed the S&P 500 this year, and is still below lows of ~$200 briefly notched in November (and earlier, in 2021). With healthy net retention rates, excellent growth/profitability balance as demonstrated by its Rule of 50 positioning, and strong deal momentum, I'm willing to look past Datadog's slightly rich valuation multiples and invest for the long haul.

data.title: C.H. Robinson Worldwide: Overlooked AI Beneficiary
data.description:
The market has been hyperventilating on AI for a while now (emphasis on while), and in my opinion, this is the most unloved and/or widely publicized bubble in recent memory.

I think both unloved and publicized attributes of this bubble can be traced to the fact that there are only a handful of beneficiaries of this bubble, and the majority of the market has shown little to no gains, even while attributing AI to be a total game-changer for our lives.

For my readers, I want to say this. Ignore the noise and be aware of the fact that there is a massive difference between building AI (which is capital-intensive and gets caught up in the bubble argument) and using AI (which is purported to increase productivity, be a net gain for the company's bottom line and can print cash)

Looking through that lens, a company that popped up on my radar would be C.H. Robinson Worldwide (CHRW).

But here is the kicker: unlike the prevailing promise in the market that companies will benefit from using AI and that the future looks bright, C.H. Robinson is using and benefiting from AI now.

C.H. Robinson Worldwide's Business Model

First, an introduction to the company and its operations.

C.H. Robinson is one of the world’s largest third-party logistics (3PL) providers. While logistics may conjure up images of trucks, ships or planes, the company's business model involves none of that (at least not directly), and they operate an asset-light model.

The company operates the network as a massive information exchange where they connect customers (businesses that need to move goods) with contract carriers (truckers, airlines, etc.). Charge the shipper a fee to move the goods, and pay the carrier a slightly lower fee to move the goods and pocket the spread.

Ultimately, the takeaway here is that customers outsource the headache of logistics to CHRW’s tech-powered network, while CHRW earns a fee on every shipment.

The proof is in the pudding

Part 1: Thriving where the industry is struggling

This is a new C.H. Robinson, and we don't use the macro environment as an excuse. We are a fundamentally different company than we were 2 years ago, illustrated by the company's consistent outperformance versus the market. But make no mistake, this consistent outperformance does not just happen, and it's not easy for others to replicate.

- David Bozeman, President, CEO and Director

It is well established by now that the goods transportation industry is in a recession and has been for some time. Many companies operating in the space are not only struggling, but some have gone bankrupt.

I pulled together a list of companies in the Logistics industry, and the results are unsurprising. Even our subject, C.H.Robinson has not fared well when you look at its revenues.

The list shows that most companies are showing flattish to negative growth, and only a few stocks/companies are showing a positive return. Strangely, among the stock return winners, C.H. Robinson showed a massive positive return while showing poor growth in its top-line. What gives?

Part 2: Improving bottom line, still rewarding shareholders

1. Despite a freight recession where revenues were down due to lower shipping rates, their earnings surged, beating analyst expectations.

2. TTM, they reported $16.5B in revenues, $1.4B in Gross profits and $0.8B in operating profits with a net income of $0.6B. Same time, 2 years ago, the numbers are $18.5B, $1.2B and $0.6B respectively, with a net income of $0.4B. This shows that margins have improved significantly.

3. They have used their robust cash flows to pay down their debt and deleverage their balance sheet. In my opinion, this is the most impressive aspect of the company. At a time when debt held by the companies in this space is spiralling and eventually ending in doom, C.H.Robinson is going in the opposite direction.

Our net debt-to-EBITDA leverage at the end of Q3 was 1.17x, down from 1.40x at the end of Q2. This financial strength is a key differentiator in our industry, giving us the ability to continue investing through the bottom of the freight cycle and further enhancing our capabilities.

- Damon Lee, CFO

4. The company has continued to engage in actions that are quite friendly to shareholders. For the latest quarter alone, they have returned $190M of cash through $115M of share repurchases and $75M of dividends. In fact, the company is an overlooked dividend champion that has consistently raised dividends over the last two decades, and they have committed to buybacks of $2B over the next three years (This buyback is on top of an existing authorization that still has 4.5M shares remaining)

Part 3: AI is the answer

A quote for a shipment from China to Charlotte, N.C., could take a worker several days because of the complexity of figuring out routes, carriers and customs paperwork. Agentic AI can compile the same quote in about an hour.

- CHRW CEO Dave Bozeman

Now comes the question that we will have to revisit. How is the company able to do this?

1. The company says it is using machine learning and generative artificial intelligence to automate tasks, with the result being higher and faster response times.

2. They have been able to automate tens of thousands of rate quotes/day and have generated about half of the company’s carrier bookings and appointments using AI.

Before AI: Able to respond to about 60% of quote requests in a day.

After AI: Ability to respond to every request

3. They have credited the technology for their drastic reduction in overall headcount numbers, while growing the customer-facing roles (the company ended June with 12.8K workers, down approximately 20% from two years earlier).

4. Since the end of 2022, C.H. Robinson has reported a net increase in productivity of over 40%.

Wrapping up, I want to leave readers with this thought. If the company is able to achieve this performance while facing a recession in its industry, how will things look when the freight market turns around?

Risks: What Could Go Wrong?

The Freight Cycle

The industry is still plagued by a recession, and the company has done a great job of navigating this through efficiency gains from Artificial Intelligence. If the global economy slows down further and ultimately results in a hard landing, shipping volumes could dry up further, and at the end of the day no amount of AI can fix a lack of customers.

Tech Disruption

While some of the legacy competitors could just be waking up to the use of AI for lean and efficient operations, the same thing may not be said of tech-first entrants such as Uber. If they build a better mousetrap, CHRW loses market share.

Currently, some of CHRW's competitors have been rated higher (in surveys conducted by Gartner) when it comes to service, support, ease of integration and deployment with better evaluation and contracting. I am not too worried yet, as it's still a minuscule sample compared to the size of their market, but if this starts to become representative of the overall market, it could become a real risk.

Valuation Compression

Out of all the risks, this is probably the one that is most obvious. By most measures, the stock appears to be overvalued, and the market continues to bid up the stock.

C.W. Robinson currently trades at a P/E of 32x, quite above the Industrials sector median, and if the market decides to treat CHRW as a boring utility rather than a tech-enabled earnings workhorse, the P/E multiple could contract much closer or even below the sector median (a 30-50% downside in that case cannot be ruled out).

In my opinion, for the stock to trade at this level and go up from here, the market is looking at one or several of the factors below, and every one of these factors could stay on for years to come.

1. Its earnings growth means its PEG ratio stands at 0.46, a highly attractive valuation metric for a company.

2. The AI-centric growth means it may not only squeeze more margins from its business but also at a prime position to capitalize when the top-line also turns upward. In this situation, the market wants to be in the driver's seat when this happens, buying into future growth.

3. The stock buybacks put a continuous bid on the stock, and in the end, it becomes a simple law of supply and demand.

The Past and the Future

I've seen gluts not followed by shortages, but I've never seen a shortage not followed by a glut.

- Nassim Nicholas Taleb

As I wrap up my analysis, I want to leave the readers with an analogy that has been in my mind for a while. Whether it plays out or not like the last time is up to anyone's guess, but I can't help shaking a very possible and potential scenario.

Rewinding to the dot-com mania, telecommunications firms became inebriated by the prospect of infinite internet growth. Multiple firms outcompeted each other to lay out fibre-optic cables across continents and under oceans. If we are looking at unlimited growth, any amount of supply is scarce. The formula was simple:

1. Build more capacity than anyone else

2. Scale faster than rivals

3. Dominate the future.

There is an estimate that up to $500B, financed mostly by debt, was invested in the infrastructure.

Of course, how many dared to ask the question whether the companies were building more capacity than the market actually needed?

The eventual bust of 2000-2002 meant the carnage was swift. Overleveraged companies went into bankruptcy, but you can probably guess where this is going next.

The fibre optics cable laid by these companies became the backbone of the internet for years to come, and the biggest beneficiaries were the users of this infrastructure, which was available at a fraction of the cost.

Fast forward to now, let's see what's happening in the market.

Trillion and billion-dollar companies are spending gobs of money, building AI models, training data centers, and acquiring GPUs with the reasoning that the future belongs to whoever accumulates the most compute. The competition, hype and overspend could be reminiscent of the dot-com era.

CEOs after CEOs, executives after executives, all have only one thing to complain about - that there is simply not enough AI infrastructure to go around.

Whether the capacity is excessive or not is only realized in hindsight, but hard not to see the parallels. The true winners may not be the ones in the infrastructure game, but it could be the companies with the massive, proprietary datasets that can plug cheap AI into their operations to unlock explosive efficiency.

As the technology becomes cheaper and gets commoditized, C.H. Robinson is already ahead of the curve in embedding them into existing logistics operations, and the improvement in models and cost means the company's moat only gets deeper and wider.

I believe C.H. Robinson is a logistics giant that is already a proven winner whose best years are ahead of it.
